channel.title: Class on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions
channel.description: Recent content in Class on ErrorCode — Error Code Reference & Solutions

item.title: [Solution] Python AttributeError — Property Issues
item.description:
AttributeError — Property Issues

An AttributeError related to property objects is raised when there are problems with property definitions, such as accessing a property on the class instead of an instance, incorrect property decorator usage, or missing getter/setter methods.

Description

Properties are managed attributes that use getter, setter, and deleter methods. They are defined using the @property decorator or the property() function. Common errors occur when properties are accessed incorrectly, when the decorator syntax is wrong, or when trying to access a property before it’s properly defined.

item.title: [Solution] Python TypeError — Cannot Create Instance
item.description:
TypeError — Cannot Create Instance

A TypeError with the message “cannot create instance” is raised when you try to instantiate a type that does not allow object creation, such as certain C-level types, abstract base classes, or types with restricted __new__ methods.

Description

Some types in Python cannot be instantiated directly. This includes types like NoneType, EllipsisType, NotImplementedType, certain abstract base classes, and types that override __new__ to prevent instantiation. This error can also occur when you try to call a type that is not actually a class.

item.title: [Solution] Python TypeError — Descriptor Issues
item.description:
TypeError — Descriptor Issues

A TypeError related to descriptors is raised when there is a mismatch between how a descriptor is defined and how it is accessed. Descriptors are objects that define __get__, __set__, or __delete__ methods, and they underpin methods, properties, and class methods in Python.

Description

Descriptors are a core Python mechanism. When you access an attribute on an instance, Python checks if the attribute’s class defines a __get__ method. Common descriptor-related errors occur when:

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2351 — This Expression Is Not Constructable
item.description:
TS2351 — This expression is not constructable

TS2351 occurs when you try to use new on a value whose type doesn’t have a construct signature. This happens when calling new on a function that isn’t a constructor, an object, or a primitive.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Using 'new' on a regular function
function greet(name: string) {
 return `Hello, ${name}`;
}
const g = new greet("Alice"); // TS2351: not constructable

// Cause 2: Using 'new' on an arrow function
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create(); // TS2351

// Cause 3: Using 'new' on a module default export that isn't a class
import utils from "./utils";
const u = new utils(); // TS2351

// Cause 4: Using 'new' on an interface
interface Factory {
 create(): Widget;
}
const f = new Factory(); // TS2351: interfaces aren't values

Solutions

Fix 1: Use a class with a constructor

// Wrong — regular function
function Greeter(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // TS2351

// Correct — use a class
class Greeter {
 name: string;
 constructor(name: string) {
 this.name = name;
 }
}
new Greeter("Alice"); // OK

Fix 2: Use factory function without ’new'

// Wrong
const create = () => ({ x: 1 });
const obj = new create();

// Correct — call without 'new'
const obj = create();

Fix 3: Import the class correctly

// Wrong — importing non-class default export
import utils from "./utils";
new utils();

// Correct — import a class
import { MyClass } from "./my-class";
new MyClass();

Fix 4: Define a construct signature for the type

interface Constructor {
 new (name: string): { name: string };
}

function createInstance(Ctor: Constructor, name: string) {
 return new Ctor(name); // OK
}

Examples

// Example 1: Using 'new' on a function stored in a variable
const initialize = function () {};
const app = new initialize(); // TS2351

// Example 2: Using 'new' on a namespace
namespace Utils {
 export function helper() {}
}
new Utils(); // TS2351

Related Errors

  • TS2349: This expression is not callable — trying to call () on non-callable.
  • TS2688: Type has no matching call signature — callable but wrong arguments.
  • TS2542: Cannot read property of type — property access issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2420 — Class incorrectly implements interface
item.description:
TS2420 — Class incorrectly implements interface

TS2420 occurs when a class that implements an interface does not provide all required members or provides members with incompatible types.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing required methods
interface Logger {
 log(message: string): void;
 error(message: string): void;
}

class ConsoleLogger implements Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 console.log(message);
 }
 // missing error() — TS2420
}

// Cause 2: Wrong property types
interface Config {
 port: number;
}

class AppConfig implements Config {
 port: string; // TS2420: 'string' is not assignable to 'number'
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Implement all required members

class ConsoleLogger implements Logger {
 log(message: string) {
 console.log(message);
 }
 error(message: string) {
 console.error(message);
 }
}

Fix 2: Use the correct types

class AppConfig implements Config {
 port: number = 3000;
}

Fix 3: Use abstract class for partial implementation

abstract class BaseLogger implements Logger {
 abstract log(message: string): void;
 abstract error(message: string): void;
}

Related Errors

  • TS2304: Class is not abstract and does not implement — abstract implementation.
  • TS2717: Non-abstract class does not implement — similar variant.
  • TS2414: Type argument not assignable — generic constraint issues.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2464 — Class Can Only Implement an Identifier
item.description:
TS2464 — A class can only implement an identifier/qualified-name

TS2464 occurs when you use implements with an expression that isn’t a valid identifier or qualified name. You can only implement named interfaces or classes, not object literals, unions, or computed values.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Implementing an object literal
class MyClass implements { name: string; age: number } { // TS2464
 name = "";
 age = 0;
}

// Cause 2: Implementing a union type
interface A { a: string; }
interface B { b: string; }
class MyClass implements A | B { // TS2464
 a = "";
}

// Cause 3: Implementing an expression
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {} // TS2464

// Cause 4: Implementing an inline type without a name
class MyClass implements { id: number } {} // TS2464

Solutions

Fix 1: Define an interface and implement it

// Wrong
class MyClass implements { name: string } {}

// Correct
interface Named {
 name: string;
}
class MyClass implements Named {
 name = "";
}

Fix 2: Only implement one interface (not a union)

// Wrong
class MyClass implements A | B {}

// Correct — implement both separately
class MyClass implements A, B {
 a = "";
 b = "";
}

Fix 3: Use interface reference instead of inline type

// Wrong
const myInterface = { x: number };
class MyClass implements myInterface {}

// Correct
interface HasX {
 x: number;
}
class MyClass implements HasX {
 x = 0;
}

Fix 4: Implement named type aliases

type Printable = { print(): void };
class MyClass implements Printable {
 print() { console.log("printed"); }
}

Examples

// Example 1: Trying to implement a generic type without instantiation
interface Container<T> { value: T; }
class MyClass implements Container { // TS2464 — missing type argument
 value = "";
}

// Fix:
class MyClass implements Container<string> {
 value = "";
}

// Example 2: Implementing a mapped type
type Options = { [key: string]: string };
class Config implements Options {} // TS2464 — can't implement mapped type directly

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements interface — implements but missing members.
  • TS2339: Property does not exist on type — accessing non-existent properties.
  • TS2741: Property is missing in type — missing required properties.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2618 — A class may only extend another class or null
item.description:
TS2618 — A class may only extend another class or null

TS2618 occurs when a class tries to extend something that is not a class (e.g., an interface, type, or object).

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Extending an interface
interface Animal {
 name: string;
}
class Dog extends Animal {} // TS2618

// Cause 2: Extending a type alias
type Base = {
 value: number;
};
class Derived extends Base {} // TS2618

// Cause 3: Extending an expression
const base = { name: "base" };
class Derived extends base {} // TS2618

How to Fix

Fix 1: Use implements instead

interface Animal {
 name: string;
}
class Dog implements Animal {
 name = "Rex";
}

Fix 2: Use class instead of interface

class Animal {
 name: string = "";
}
class Dog extends Animal {
 name = "Rex";
}

Fix 3: Use mixin pattern

type Constructor<T = {}> = new (...args: any[]) => T;
function Timestamped<TBase extends Constructor>(Base: TBase) {
 return class extends Base {
 createdAt = new Date();
 };
}

Related Errors

  • TS2320: Interface cannot extend — interface extension.
  • TS2461: Interface incorrectly extends — interface type conflict.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — class implementation.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2717 — Non-abstract class does not implement
item.description:
TS2717 — Non-abstract class ‘X’ does not implement inherited abstract member

TS2717 occurs when a concrete (non-abstract) class extends an abstract class or implements an interface but fails to provide implementations for all abstract members.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Missing abstract method implementation
abstract class Shape {
 abstract area(): number;
}

class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 // missing area() — TS2717
}

// Cause 2: Incomplete interface implementation
interface Repository {
 find(id: number): any;
 save(entity: any): void;
}

class UserRepository implements Repository {
 find(id: number) {
 return null;
 }
 // missing save() — TS2717
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Implement all abstract members

class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 area(): number {
 return Math.PI * this.radius ** 2;
 }
}

Fix 2: Use abstract in concrete class

abstract class Circle extends Shape {
 radius: number;
 constructor(r: number) {
 super();
 this.radius = r;
 }
 // area() left abstract — subclass must implement
}

Related Errors

  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface implementation.
  • TS2720: Class extends abstract — abstract member conflict.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined member.

item.title: [Solution] TypeScript TS2720 — Class 'X' defines instance member 'Y'
item.description:
TS2720 — Class ‘X’ defines instance member ‘Y’ but previously required static member

TS2720 occurs when a class extends an abstract class but implements a member with the wrong static/instance modifier.

Common Causes

// Cause 1: Instance vs static mismatch
abstract class Base {
 abstract static create(): Base;
}

class Derived extends Base {
 create(): Base { // TS2720: instance member, expected static
 return new Derived();
 }
}

// Cause 2: Wrong modifier
abstract class Logger {
 abstract log(msg: string): void;
}

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
 static log(msg: string) {} // TS2720: static, expected instance
}

How to Fix

Fix 1: Match the static/instance modifier

abstract class Base {
 static create(): Base;
}

class Derived extends Base {
 static create(): Base {
 return new Derived();
 }
}

Fix 2: Use correct instance method

class ConsoleLogger extends Logger {
 log(msg: string) {
 console.log(msg);
 }
}

Related Errors

  • TS2717: Non-abstract class does not implement — missing implementation.
  • TS2420: Class incorrectly implements — interface mismatch.
  • TS2304: Cannot find name — undefined member.

item.title: ClassCastException
item.description:
What This Error Means

A ClassCastException is thrown when you try to cast an object to a type that it isn’t compatible with. Groovy performs runtime type checking for explicit casts using the as keyword.

Common Causes

  • Casting incompatible types
  • Assumptions about return types from methods
  • Generic type erasure causing wrong assumptions
  • Interoperating with Java collections

How to Fix

// WRONG: Invalid cast
def x = "hello"
def n = x as int // ClassCastException

// CORRECT: Check type before casting
def x = "hello"
if (x instanceof Integer) {
 def n = x as int
}

// WRONG: Wrong generic type assumption
List<Integer> numbers = [1, 2, 3]
numbers.add("four") // may fail at runtime with ClassCastException

// CORRECT: Use proper typing
List<Integer> numbers = [1, 2, 3]
numbers.add(4) // correct

Examples

// Example 1: String to Integer
def s = "hello"
int n = s as int // ClassCastException

// Example 2: Wrong collection type
def list = [1, 2, 3] as ArrayList
list.add("four") // ClassCastException when iterating

// Example 3: Interface cast
class Dog { void bark() {} }
def animal = new Dog()
def runnable = animal as Runnable // ClassCastException

Related Errors

  • NullPointerException
  • MissingMethodException: No signature of method

item.title: ClassCastException
item.description:
What This Error Means

A ClassCastException is thrown when you try to cast an object to a type that it isn’t. This happens with explicit asInstanceOf calls or when the compiler infers the wrong type due to type erasure.

Common Causes

  • Explicit cast with asInstanceOf to wrong type
  • Type erasure in generic collections
  • Incorrect assumption about collection element types
  • Java interop returning unexpected types

How to Fix

// WRONG: Unsafe cast
val x: Any = "hello"
val n: Int = x.asInstanceOf[Int] // ClassCastException

// CORRECT: Use pattern matching or isInstanceOf check
val x: Any = "hello"
x match {
 case n: Int => println(n)
 case s: String => println(s)
}

// WRONG: Type erasure issue
val list: List[Any] = List(1, "two", 3.0)
val strings: List[String] = list.asInstanceOf[List[String]] // ClassCastException

// CORRECT: Use collect or filter
val strings: List[String] = list.collect { case s: String => s }

Examples

// Example 1: Direct cast
val obj: Any = "hello"
val num = obj.asInstanceOf[Int] // ClassCastException

// Example 2: Generic type erasure
val map = Map("a" -> List(1, 2))
val wrong = map.asInstanceOf[Map[String, List[String]]] // may fail

// Example 3: Java interop
import java.util.Collections
val javaList = Collections.emptyList[String]()
val scalaList = javaList.asInstanceOf[List[String]] // ClassCastException

Related Errors

  • NullPointerException
  • MatchError

item.title: Metaclass error
item.description:
What This Error Means

A Metaclass error occurs when Groovy cannot find a method or property through the metaclass system, or when there’s an issue with dynamic method resolution. This is related to Groovy’s dynamic nature and metaclass programming.

Common Causes

  • Missing method on metaclass
  • Invalid metaclass modification
  • Category not properly registered
  • Method not found in category or metaclass

How to Fix

// WRONG: Using non-existent metaclass method
String.metaClass.nonExistentMethod = { -> "hello" }
"hello".nonExistentMethod() // works, but:
def s = "hello"
s.someOtherMethod() // MissingMethodException

// CORRECT: Define and use consistently
String.metaClass.greet = { -> "Hello from metaclass" }
"hello".greet() // "Hello from metaclass"

// WRONG: Category not properly used
class StringUtils {
 static String shout(String self) { self.toUpperCase() + "!" }
}
// String.shout() // doesn't work without use(StringUtils)

// CORRECT: Use category in scope
use(StringUtils) {
 println "hello".shout() // HELLO!
}

Examples

// Example 1: Method not in metaclass
class MyClass {}
MyClass.metaClass.newMethod = { -> "new" }
def obj = new MyClass()
obj.oldMethod() // MissingMethodException

// Example 2: Category scope issue
class Helper {
 static String help(String s) { "helped: $s" }
}
// Outside use(Helper), help() not available

// Example 3: Metaclass conflict
String.metaClass.toString = { -> "custom" } // overrides toString
def s = "hello"
println s // "custom" - may cause unexpected behavior

Related Errors

  • MissingMethodException: No signature of method
  • MissingPropertyException: No such property
